model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current run improved optimization without changing model expressiveness. A single extra convolution-pool stage is a simple capacity/inductive-bias upgrade for CIFAR-10 that still keeps the model compact, reduces the final spatial map before the MLP head, and should fit the fixed 256-step budget without adding much script complexity.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The depth increase paid off, so the next simple capacity move is to widen the convolution stack slightly. Raising the base width from 12 to 16 strengthens feature extraction across all three stages while keeping the architecture unchanged and should still fit the short 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The widened 3-stage CNN is now strong enough that cleaner decoupled weight decay is a sensible next optimization change. Swapping Adam to AdamW is a minimal edit that often improves short-budget CNN training stability and generalization without disturbing the successful schedule or architecture.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"AdamW regressed from the best visible loss, so the next step should remove that unsuccessful optimizer-family change and return to the stronger Adam baseline before exploring other axes. This is the lowest-risk correction and preserves the gains from the depth, width, and schedule edits.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The architecture and schedule changes produced most of the gain, so the next low-complexity move is to retune the step size around the current Adam setting. A moderate increase to 1.5e-3 should test whether the improved 3-stage, wider CNN can make better use of the short fixed-step budget without changing any other behavior.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last gain came purely from raising the learning rate, which suggests the current Adam plus cosine setup is still under-driving the fixed 256-step budget. A small follow-up increase to 2e-3 is the most direct next test and preserves the rest of the now-strong training recipe.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Both recent improvements came from higher learning rates, so the most efficient next experiment is another controlled increase rather than changing a different axis. Moving from 2e-3 to 2.5e-3 keeps the recipe intact while testing whether the short 256-step budget still benefits from faster optimization.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move to 2.5e-3 overshot the optimum and regressed from the best visible loss, so the next step should revert to the stronger 2e-3 setting before exploring other axes. This keeps the best-performing recipe intact and avoids compounding a known bad change.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The current recipe appears optimization-limited under a fixed 256-step budget. Increasing batch size to 128 is a simple way to expose the model to substantially more training examples within the same number of updates, which can improve validation loss here without disturbing the architecture, optimizer, or scheduler.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The large jump from batch 64 to 128 indicates this workload is strongly limited by how many training examples the model sees within the fixed 256-step budget. Increasing to 256 is the cleanest next test because it roughly doubles exposure again, pushing training past one full epoch while keeping the successful architecture, optimizer, and schedule unchanged.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After the large-batch jump to 256, the optimization regime changed substantially and the previous 2.5e-3 result at smaller batch is no longer the right reference point. A modest learning-rate increase is the cleanest next test because larger batches often support a larger step size while preserving the now-strong architecture and data-exposure gains.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger batch continues to respond well to higher learning rates, so the next high-value experiment is another modest step upward rather than changing the recipe. Raising the rate from 2.5e-3 to 3e-3 tests whether the current large-batch setup can still convert faster optimization into lower validation loss within the same fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current large-batch regime is still improving as learning rate increases, so the highest-value next probe is another upward step rather than changing a different part of the recipe. Moving from 3e-3 to 4e-3 is a meaningful but still controlled increase that may extract more progress from the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Learning-rate increases in the current batch-256 regime have continued to improve validation loss, so the most informative next step is to keep moving along that same axis. Raising the rate from 4e-3 to 5e-3 is still a modest one-step change and may further improve early-budget optimization before instability appears.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 6e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The optimization trajectory is still improving as the learning rate rises in the batch-256 setting, so the most efficient next experiment is one more controlled increase on the same axis. Moving from 5e-3 to 6e-3 is a small enough step to stay safe while still testing whether the current recipe has not yet reached its best early-budget step size.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"At this point the recipe is training aggressively but still only sees a short effective training horizon, so the current weight decay may be slightly over-regularizing. Halving it to 5e-5 is a minimal regularization change that tests whether the model can convert the strong optimization setup into a lower validation loss without altering the successful batch size or learning-rate regime.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Lowering weight decay regressed from the best visible loss, so the next step should remove that unsuccessful regularization change and restore the stronger 1e-4 setting. This preserves the best-performing large-batch, high-learning-rate recipe before spending the remaining budget on other axes.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The biggest gains in this run came from increasing batch size, which directly raises dataset exposure under the fixed 256-step budget. With only a couple of steps left, moving from 256 to 512 is the highest-upside remaining test because it could push the training loop through multiple full passes of the data while keeping the proven architecture and optimizer settings intact.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 6e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The final remaining shot should exploit the strongest pattern in the run: larger batches consistently benefited from larger learning rates. With batch size now at 512 and a big jump in validation loss already achieved, increasing the learning rate from 6e-3 to 8e-3 is the highest-upside final test for extracting more progress from the same fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}
